coredata.dc:title: Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth freestanding flexible anodes for high-performance lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2018 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Ji
Tian, Ran
Guo, Yiping
Du, Qi
Dong, Wen
Guo, Runjiang
Fu, Xiuwu
Guan, Lin
Liu, Hezhou
coredata.dc:description: Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth, prepared via the coherent growth of Zn2SnO4 on pre-functionalized carbon cloth, was employed as a freestanding and binder-free anode for lithium-ion batteries. The 2D Zn2SnO4 nano-plates were uniformly anchored on the surface of 1D carbon cloth to form a hierarchical structure, providing the fast Li-ion and electron exchange path and reducing the volume change during the Li-ion planting/stripping. The binder-free electrode delivered significantly enhanced electrochemical performance by maintaining a reversible capacity of 1510 mA h/g after 60 discharge-charge cycles at a current density of 100 mA/g compared with that of the Zn2SnO4. Systematic structural and electrochemical characterizations suggested that the synergistic effect between Zn2SnO4 nanoplates and carbon cloth was responsible for the high electrochemical performance. Therefore, this study would be of significance for promoting the application of binary transition metal oxides in lithium-ion batteries.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the formation process of Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites.
The EDS pattern of the pre-functionalized carbon cloth.
XRD patterns of plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites, pure carbon cloth, and plate-like Zn2SnO4.
SEM images of (a)–(b) plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites, and TEM image of Zn2SnO4 nanoplate (c).
Cyclic voltammograms of (a) plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites and (b) pure Zn2SnO4.
Cyclic voltage-specific capacity profiles of (a) flexible electrodes and (b) traditional electrodes.
Cyclic performance of electrodes with coulombic efficiency of flexible electrodes.
The rate performance of flexible and traditional electrodes.
EIS spectra of electrodes with an equivalent circuit model.
The schematic diagram of work mechanism of active materials.

head.ce:title: Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth freestanding flexible anodes for high-performance lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Warwick, Gibbet Hill Road, Coventry CV4 7AL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth, prepared via the coherent growth of Zn2SnO4 on pre-functionalized carbon cloth, was employed as a freestanding and binder-free anode for lithium-ion batteries. The 2D Zn2SnO4 nano-plates were uniformly anchored on the surface of 1D carbon cloth to form a hierarchical structure, providing the fast Li-ion and electron exchange path and reducing the volume change during the Li-ion planting/stripping. The binder-free electrode delivered significantly enhanced electrochemical performance by maintaining a reversible capacity of 1510 mA h/g after 60 discharge-charge cycles at a current density of 100 mA/g compared with that of the Zn2SnO4. Systematic structural and electrochemical characterizations suggested that the synergistic effect between Zn2SnO4 nanoplates and carbon cloth was responsible for the high electrochemical performance. Therefore, this study would be of significance for promoting the application of binary transition metal oxides in lithium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in portable electronic devices and electric vehicles due to their high energy density and light weight [1–3]. The main commercial LIBs are those with graphite as the anode material. The lithiation reaction type of graphite electrode is called ‘rocking chair’, in which Li+ from cathode is inserted into the space between two sheets of graphite [4]. The negligible volume change during the lithiation-delithiation process benefits the high capacity retention in this type of electrodes [5]. However, the low theoretical capacity of graphite (372 mA h/g) restricts the use of commercial LIBs on a large scale. As a potential alternative, binary transition metal oxides have drawn increasing attention due to their generally high reversible capacities and outstanding theoretical capacities [6–11]. Of these, zinc stannate (Zn2SnO4) is a promising candidate with high theoretical capacity (1410 mA h/g) and low cost [12–16]. The lithiation reaction type is accompanied by the production of metal nanocrystals and Li2O, which inevitably cause non-negligible volume expansion [17]. The general approach to reducing/controlling the volume variation is to synthesize composite materials based on the transition metal oxides in combination with carbon materials. In a conventional synthesis method, the slurry, made of active materials, binder and conductive agent, is coated onto metallic collectors. The mechanical combination between active materials and collectors is not efficient enough to transfer the strain caused by volume change during the charge-discharge cycles. In addition, the water-soluble binder is toxic.

In recent years, freestanding and flexible electrodes based on soft and bendable substrates have been developed for LIBs and supercapacitors with merits in electrical conductivity and capacity retention [18–20]. This kind of substrates not only enhances the electron mobility and electrical conductivity but also directly buffers the volume change in active materials [21–23]. For example, the substrates composed of metal oxides (i.e., SnO2 and V2O5) and carbon materials have been extensively used to enhance the electrochemical performance [21, 24-26]. The bimetal oxides, such as Zn2SnO4, CoSnO3, and NiCo2O4, are hihly desirable as the anode materials for LIBs because they can exhibit some special functions as a result of synergism between the metal oxide components, such as an adjustable working potential and increased electronic conductivity [27-30]. Zn2SnO4/graphene (carbon) composites have received much attention recently [31-34]. However, still, no reports exist about the flexible Zn2SnO4-carbon composites.

Bearing this in mind, carbon cloth was chosen in this study as a carbon matrix owning to its high mechanical property and outstanding electrical conductivity. Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites (ZCCs) were prepared successfully via a modified process combined with pre-functionalization and hydrothermal method. The ZCC electrodes exhibited much better electrochemical performance compared with traditional electrodes. Systematic electrochemical characterization and analysis were carried out to uncover the mechanism responsible for the enhancement.

The carbon cloth was washed using ultrasonic cleaning for 1 h in cleaning agent (volume ratio of acetone and ethanol was 1:1), and then for another 1 h in deionized water. The cleaning process was repeated several times. A piece of carbon cloth, of a size of 2 ∗ 2 cm was put into 100 mL of HNO3 and transferred into an ultrasonic bath where the concentrated nitric acid (68 wt%) was used as an oxidizer to functionalize the surfaces of carbon cloth microwires. After 4 h, the carbon cloth was taken out and washed with deionized water and ethanol several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites
ce:section.ce:para: The precursor suspension of plate-like Zn2SnO4 nanocrystals was prepared with Zn(Ac)2 and Na2SnO3 solutions at a molar ratio of 3:1. The as-prepared precursor was transferred into a 50 -mL Teflon stainless autoclave. Then, a piece of pre-functionalized carbon cloth on a glass substrate was vertically immersed into the precursor. Afterward, the Teflon stainless autoclave was heated and kept in an oven at 260 °C for 24 h. The plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites were obtained after washing with deionized water and ethanol.

In addition, this pre-functionalization process was also used to synthesize the octahedron-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites with a different precursor. The preparation process, x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, and scanning electron microscopy(SEM) images of the as-prepared sample are detailed in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process to make electrodes and assemble LIBs
ce:section.ce:para: The flexible electrodes were simply obtained by cutting the plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites. The loading mass of the active materials was about 2 mg cm−2, which was equal to the difference between the composites and pre-functionalized carbon cloth. As a comparison, traditional electrodes with pure plate-like Zn2SnO4 as active materials were also synthesized using a slurry-coating method. The slurries were prepared by mixing active materials, conductive agent carbon black and binder with a weight ratio of 8:1:1, and then coated on copper foils. The mass of active materials was the same as those of flexible electrodes. The traditional electrodes were prepared after drying in a vacuum oven at 110 °C for 12 h. The assembling processes of LIBs for the two kinds of electrodes were the same. CR2025-type coin cells were assembled in the vacuum glove box full of Ar with the Celgard2325 micro-porous membrane as the separator.

Carbon cloth, pure plate-like Zn2SnO4 and composites were identified using XRD ranging from 20°–60°. Their morphologies were investigated by using SEM. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) of ZCC and traditional electrodes were tested from 3 to 0.01 V at a scan rate of 0.2 mV/s. The charge-discharge curves were plotted with a voltage range of 3–0.01 V at a current density of 100 mA/g (about 200 mA/cm2). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the electrodes was conducted at a frequency range of 100–0.01 Hz after 10 charge-discharge cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation process of Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The formation process of Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The surfaces of carbon cloth microwires possessed many functional groups after pre-functionalization with HNO3, which was confirmed by the existence of oxygen in the Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) pattern (Fig. 2 ). These functional groups provided anchor sites for Zn2+, leading to the in situ formation of Zn2SnO4 nanocrystals during hydrothermal treatments. The chemical equations involved in the synthesis of Zn2SnO4 are showed in Eqs. (1)–(4) [35,36].
(1)
Zn
2 +
+ 2
OH −
→ Zn
OH
2
(2)
Zn
OH
2
+
SnO 3
2 −
+ 3
H 2
O → ZnSn
OH
6
+ 2
OH −
(3)
2 ZnSn
OH
6
→
Zn 2
SnO 4
+
SnO 2
+ 3
H 2
O
(4)
SnO 2
+ 2
OH −
→
SnO 3
2 −
+
H 2
O

The XRD patterns of pure carbon cloth, plate-like Zn2SnO4, and Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites are shown in Fig. 3 . The morphology of plate-like Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites is shown in Fig. 4 . The nanoplates were vertically and uniformly dispersed on the surfaces of carbon cloth microwires. Figures S1–S2 shows that octahedral nanocrystals with uniform sizes were also anchored on the surfaces of the carbon cloth. Therefore, the results suggested the uniform growth of Zn2SnO4 nanocrystals on the surfaces of carbon cloth microwires. Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites with various Zn2SnO4 morphology were synthesized using the method with different precursor solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of flexible and traditional electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the reactions of ZnO and SnO2 during the charge-discharge process, the reactions of Zn2SnO4 were proposed and are shown in Eqs. (5)–(10) [37,38].
(5)
4
Li +
+ 4
e −
+
Zn 2
SnO 4
→ Sn + 2 ZnO + 2
Li 2
O
(6)
8
Li +
+ 8
e −
+
Zn 2
SnO 4
→ Sn + 2 Zn + 4
Li 2
O
(7)
xLi +
+
xe −
+ Zn ↔
Li x
Zn ,
x ≤ 1
(8)
yLi +
+
ye −
+ Sn ↔
Li y
Sn ,
y ≤ 4.4
(9)
2
Li +
+ 2
e −
+ ZnO ↔ Zn +
Li 2
O
(10)
4
Li +
+ 4
e −
+
SnO 2
↔ Sn + 2
Li 2
O

CV curves were poltted to study the mechanism of charge-discharge cycles, they are shown in Fig. 5 . In the first cycle (Fig. 5(a)), an obvious reduction peak was present near 0.45 V, which was related to the formation of solid electrolyte interfaces (SEIs) on the surface of Zn2SnO4. It is generally accepted that this reaction is not reversible and should be responsible for the large irreversible capacity loss during the first cycle. After the first cycle, the reaction for the remaining cycles were more reversible/stable as indicated by the similar shape of their curves. In the second cycle, the reduction peaks around 1.02 V were ascribed to the reduction of Zn2SnO4 to form Li2O (Eqs. (5) and (6)). The following reduction peak under 0.55 V corresponded to the alloying between M (M = Sn, and Zn) and Li (Eqs. (7) and (8)). The oxidation peaks around 0.45 V and 1.23 V corresponded to the dealloying process of LixSn or LixZn alloy (Eqs. (7) and (8)), and the transition of Li2O to SnOx or ZnOx (Eqs. (9) and (10)). It should be noted that Eqs. 9 and (10) are usually considered to be irreversible, but the Zn2SnO4 nanoparticles had the size effect to decrease the activation energy of this electrochemical reaction and enable its reversibility. Both the reduction and oxidation peaks were quite similar/consistent with the peaks on the curves of bareZn2SnO4, suggesting the same electrochemical reaction during the charging and discharging processes.

Fig. 6 shows cyclic voltage-specific capacity profiles of the two kinds of electrodes. The initial discharge capacity of the flexible electrodes was 3980 mA h/g, which was much higher than that of the traditional electrodes (1609 mA h/g). The corresponding coulombic efficiency was 78.4%, and 68.1%, respectively. In addition, a discharge plateau appeared at ~0.5 V in the first cycle, as shown in Fig. 6(a), which was consistent with the cathodic peak shown in Fig. 5(a).

The cyclic performance of electrodes was tested with a current density of 100 mA/g and their curves are shown in Fig. 7 . It was clearly seen that reversible capacities of the flexible electrodes were much higher than those of the traditional electrodes. The irreversible capacity loss in the first cycle was due to the formation of SEI layer. In addition, the coulombic efficiency of the flexible electrodes was maintained above 90% from the second cycle to the 60th cycle. During charge-discharge cycles, the reversible capacities of the flexible electrodes were overall stable except for the capacity decay in the first two cycles, while it was completely different with the traditional electrodes where large volume changes took place during the charge-discharge process. For the traditional electrodes, the rapid decay of capacities occurred since the 20th cycle and dropped to 668 mA h/g after the cycles. In contrast, the capacity of the flexible electrodes was still 1510 mA h/g. The specific capacity of the composite materials was also higher than the bare carbon cloth (Fig. S3), indicating that the cyclic stability is significantly improved after the combination with the carbon cloth.

The rate performance of the flexible and slurry-coated electrodes is shown in charge-discharges curves (Fig. 8 ). The reversible capacities of the flexible electrodes were much higher than those of the traditional electrodes at different current densities. At the current density of 1000 mA/g, the former could still deliver 536 mA h/g while the latter was matained at 24 mA h/g. When the current density drropped to 100 mA/g, the reversible capacities of flexible electrodes returned to 1013 mA h/g. With the same current density, the capacity of the traditional electrodes increased to 136 mA h/g. Therefore, the rate performance of Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites electrodes was significantly enhanced.

EIS measurements were conducted to further investigate electrochemical reaction kinetics of electrodes, they are exhibited in Fig. 9 (with inset of an equivalent circuit model). A semicircle was found in the high frequency and a Warburg line in low and medium frequencies. In the circuit model, Rct represented the charge transfer resistance between the electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Rf was related to the SEI layer impedance [39]. The diameter of the semicircle for the flexible electrodes was much smaller than that for the traditional electrodes, which implying that the corresponding resistance for flexible electrodes during charge transfer was lower and thus improved their electrochemical performance.

The working mechanism of active materials during the charge-discharge process is shown in Fig. 10 . The improvement was attributed to several factors. First, carbon cloth as a plastic matrix helped in releasing and transferring some volume changes during the charge-discharge process. The carbon cloth acted as a buffer for the initial press in the assembling process. Second, Zn2SnO4 nanoplates vertically anchored on the surface of microwires helped in forming a hierarchical structure, which increased the total contact area between active materials and electrolyte. Third, the connection between Zn2SnO4 nanoplates and carbon cloth microwires was a chemical bond rather than a simple physical adsorption. The excellent binding of the interface largely enhanced the electronic conductivity of active materials. Therefore, the electrochemical performance of ZCC electroded was much better than that of previously reported Zn2SnO4 [40], ZnSnO3 [41,42] and SnO2 [43,44] -based anodes.

Zn2SnO4-carbon cloth composites with different regular shapes were obtained via a modified process combined with pre-functionalization and hydrothermal method. Zn2SnO4-based composites were first used as flexible and freestanding electrodes without any conductive agent and binder. Systematic electrochemical measurements suggested that the flexible electrodes delivered much better electrochemical performance compared with the traditional electrodes. The 2D Zn2SnO4 nano-plate and 1D carbon fiber formed the hierarchical structure which provided the fast Li-ion and electron exchange path, and reduced the volume change during the Li-ion planting/stripping. After 60 charge-discharge cycles, the flexible electrodes still matained a capacity of 1510 mA h/g. They also delivered a capacity of 536 mA h/g at the current density of 1000 mA/g. The large increase in cyclic stability and improved rate performance were due to the synergistic reaction between Zn2SnO4 nanocrystals and carbon cloth. The ZCC flexible electrodes not only simplified the preparation of electrodes but also significantly enhanced the electrochemical performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support of the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11474199 and 51332009). The Instrumental Analysis Center of Shanghai Jiao Tong University is sincerely acknowledged for assisting with the relevant analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2018.06.056.
